coredata.dc:title: Homostructured graphene oxide-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite-based membranes with tunable interlayer spacing for the purification of butanol
coredata.dc:creator: Lecaros, Rumwald Leo G.
Matira, Andrea R.
Tayo, Lemmuel L.
Hung, Wei-Song
Hu, Chien-Chieh
Tsai, Hui-An
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
Lai, Juin-Yih
coredata.dc:description: The two-dimensional graphene oxide (GO) shares similar structure and functional groups with the zero-dimensional oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs) but differs in size. Herein, homostructured membranes with GO and OGQDs were prepared through the pressure-assisted self-assembly method. The OGQDs loading were varied to adjust the interlayer spacing between GO nanosheets. The fabricated GO-OGQDs composite membranes were characterized accordingly to identify the interactions between the two carbon-based nanomaterials and its effect on the pervaporative dehydration of n-butanol. A stable nanocomposite lamella was achieved through π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding. The membranes showed an excellent performance in pervaporation of 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C. The pristine GO and GO-OGQD100 composite membranes resulted in a total flux of 760 gm-2h−1 and 945 gm-2h−1, respectively. At 100 ppm OGQD loading, the composite membrane also resulted with a high-water concentration in permeate of 99.99 wt%. The augmented performance was due to the alteration of the hydrophilic transport channels on GO by OGQD integration. This helped to increase the water flux and blocks the n-butanol molecules to permeate. The GO-OGQD100 composite membrane provides good performance even in long-term operation. The results herein provide an understanding in preparing nanocomposite materials with similar chemical structures that differ in size. Furthermore, the successful purification of butanol could be done through an environmentally friendly membrane separation process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Homostructured layer
Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Butanol
Pervaporation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs)
Pervaporation separation performance of the composite membrane
Pervaporation separation performance with butanol isomer/water mixtures
Long-term operating performance of the composite membrane
Comparison of pervaporation performances with other membranes reported

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to sincerely thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108-2218-E-033-007-MY3 and MOST 109-2811-E-011-513) for financially supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (a) the pristine GO and homostructured nanocomposite membranes with increasing OGQD loading and their separated regions of the spectra (b) from 4000 to 2000 cm−1 and (c) from 2000 to 650 cm−1.
XPS analysis of (a) C1s narrow scan spectrum of OGQD and (b) wide scan spectra of GO and GO-OGQD100 membranes.
FE-SEM cross-sectional images of (a) GO and homostructured nanocomposite membranes with increasing loading of OGQDs: (b) GO-OGQD10, (c) GO-OGQD30, (d) GO-OGQD50, (e) GO-OGQD100, and (f) GO-OGQD200.
A schematic representation of the integration of OGQD particles within the GO nanosheets.

XRD spectra of pristine GO and GO-OGQD homostructured nanocomposite membranes in (a) dry state and (b) wet state conditions. The (c) calculated and changes in d-spacing of GO and GO-OGQD homostructured nanocomposite. The wet state conditions were done by pre-soaking the membranes in 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture for 24 h.
Effect of OGQD loading amount in the pervaporation dehydration performance of the membranes in separating 90/10 wt% n-BuOH/water mixture at 25 °C.
Schematic representation of the transport process of water and n-butanol molecules within the graphene oxide nanosheets in the (a) presence and (b) absence of OGQD.

Effect of feed temperature on the homostructured nanocomposite GO-OGQD100 membrane in separating 90/10 wt% n-BuOH/water mixture: (a) Total flux and water concentration in permeate and (b) water and n-BuOH individual flux.
Effect of feed composition on the homostructured nanocomposite GO-OGQD100 membrane at 25 °C: Total flux and water concentration in permeate.
Pervaporation separation performance of the homostructured nanocomposite GO-OGQD100 membrane in separating four butanol isomer/water mixtures at 25 °C.
Long-term operation performance of the homostructured nanocomposite GO-OGQD100 membrane in separating 90/10 wt% n-BuOH/water mixture at 25 °C.
Comparison of membranes used to separate n-butanol/water through pervaporation.

head.ce:title: Homostructured graphene oxide-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite-based membranes with tunable interlayer spacing for the purification of butanol

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology
National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: R.L.G. Lecaros and A.R. Matira are co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The successful tuning of the GO interlayer space was possible by using OGQDs. The integration of OGQDs was also found to improve the separation efficiency by blocking the transport of alcohol molecules and allows the faster transport of water molecules.

The two-dimensional graphene oxide (GO) shares similar structure and functional groups with the zero-dimensional oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs) but differs in size. Herein, homostructured membranes with GO and OGQDs were prepared through the pressure-assisted self-assembly method. The OGQDs loading were varied to adjust the interlayer spacing between GO nanosheets. The fabricated GO-OGQDs composite membranes were characterized accordingly to identify the interactions between the two carbon-based nanomaterials and its effect on the pervaporative dehydration of n-butanol. A stable nanocomposite lamella was achieved through π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding. The membranes showed an excellent performance in pervaporation of 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water mixture at 25 °C. The pristine GO and GO-OGQD100 composite membranes resulted in a total flux of 760 gm-2h−1 and 945 gm-2h−1, respectively. At 100 ppm OGQD loading, the composite membrane also resulted with a high-water concentration in permeate of 99.99 wt%. The augmented performance was due to the alteration of the hydrophilic transport channels on GO by OGQD integration. This helped to increase the water flux and blocks the n-butanol molecules to permeate. The GO-OGQD100 composite membrane provides good performance even in long-term operation. The results herein provide an understanding in preparing nanocomposite materials with similar chemical structures that differ in size. Furthermore, the successful purification of butanol could be done through an environmentally friendly membrane separation process.

ce:section.ce:para: The consumption of fossil fuel is still one of the world’s greatest concern due to its limited source and impact on environment [1]. Biofuels produced from biomass or conversion of feedstock using the bacteria Clostridium sp. strains have shown potential alternative fuel resource [2,3]. Biobutanol was found to have an energy value similar to that of the gasoline and greater than bioethanol [4]. Thus, it is still interesting to treat and purify butanol from the fermentation process. The purification process involves certain limitations from low production yield, recovery, and separation of butanol and water at azeotrope point. A way to resolve the purification of biobutanol is through membrane-based separation technology which is an environmentally-safe procedure for solvent dehydration. An efficient membrane-based technology that can effectively separate liquid mixtures of close boiling point and azeotropes is pervaporation (PV). In hydrophilic PV, water is preferentially permeated across the membrane to concentrate the organic solvent feed mixtures. Thus, hydrophilic membranes prepared using synthetic or natural polymers are commonly fabricated [5–7]. However, these hydrophilic polymers must be modified in such a way that it can withstand excessive swelling of the membrane matrix leading to the decline of selectivity. As an alternative choice, hybrid composite membranes made with organic and inorganic substances are used to take advantages of the properties of both substances together [8–12]. Currently, graphene oxide (GO) composite membranes shows exceptional separation efficiency across various membrane separation technology like ultrafiltration [13,14], nanofiltration [15–17], gas separation [18–20], and pervaporation [21–23].

GO is a 2-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial that the selectivity in membrane-based technology is highly dependent on the interlayer spacing of nanosheets. GO-based membranes are known to be unstable at high-water feed composition because of the abundant hydrophilic oxygen containing functional groups that tends to form strong hydrogen bond with water molecules leading to the disintegration of the nanosheets. Thus, GO is also modified by introducing intercalating agents and cross-linkers to stabilize the nanosheets [24]. Another strategy is to chemically or hydrothermally reduce the GO but this process could lead to the formation of non-selective defects and relatively decrease the hydrophilicity that negatively impacts the separation efficiency of the membrane [25–27]. GO-based membranes in separation of butanol/water mixtures via PV have gained extensive research throughout the years. For lamellar GO membranes separating 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water, Tsou et al. pointed out that the pressure-assisted deposition of GO on polyacrylonitrile (PAN) supporting membranes is the most efficient method to prepare the lamellar layers that resulted to a 99.6 wt% of water in permeate with a permeation flux of 2.54 kg m-2h−1 at 30 °C [28]. While Chen et al. showed a vacuum-assisted deposition of GO on AAO substrate providing a thin-layer of about 600 nm and gave a permeation flux of 3.10 kg m-2h−1 with a water in permeate of 96.2 wt% at 50 °C [29]. Liu et al. fabricated a layer-by-layer assembly of chitosan (CS) and GO through spin-coating on a PAN substrate and showed a high flux of 4.69 kg m-2h−1 and water in permeate of ∼ 99.8 wt% at 70 °C [30]. Currently, no study has shown the use of quantum dots to tune the interlayer space of GO especially in alcohol/water separation and specifically for separation of n-butanol/water mixtures. However, the creation of reduced GO (rGO) and oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs) homostructured fillers potentially were incorporated into a polymeric membrane matrix alleviates the separation performance of the mixed matrix membrane for small molecular weight alcohols (e.g., methanol and ethanol) during the dehydration process via PV [23]. Hence, it is proposed that the OGQDs could also be used to tune the interlayer space distance between GO nanosheets for a composite membrane with a purely inorganic separation layer.

It is hypothesized in this study that the similarity in the structure and chemical functional groups of OGQDs with GO could potentially affect the interlayer spacing due to π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding between the carbon-based nanomaterials. The composite membrane with the GO-OGQD selective layer is subjected to the dehydration of butanol/water mixture correlating with the changes happened in the interlayer space distance.

Citric acid anhydrous (CA, MW 192.13 g mol−1, ≥99.5%) and sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH, MW 40.0 g mol−1, ≥98%) were both purchased from Showa Chemical Industry Co., Ltd (Japan). Graphene oxide (GO) powder was purchased from Angstron Materials, Inc. (Ohio, United States). Hydrophilic polyamide Nylon-6,6 microfiltration membranes with 0.22 µm pore size were acquired from Anow (China). Deionized water (DI water) was purified using the water purification system from Lotun Science, Co., Ltd. (Taiwan) with a minimum resistivity of 18.0 M Ω • cm. n-Butanol (99.9 %) was purchased from Echo Chemical Co., Ltd. (Taiwan), sec-butanol (99.0%) was from Acros Organics (Belgium), iso-butanol (≥99.0% GC) was from J.T. Baker (Avantor Performance Materials PA, USA), and tert-butanol (≥99.0% GC) was from Tedia Co., Inc. (Fairfield, OH, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The partial pyrolysis of citric acid to produce oxygen-passivated graphene quantum dots (OGQD) was done in accordance with Dong et al. [31] method with slight modification. Briefly, it started with adding 2 g of CA in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave flask and subjected in an oven at 210 °C for 3 h to undergo pyrolysis. The next step is the addition of 10 mg/mL NaOH into the liquefied CA until the pH is equal to 7. The solution was then subjected to dialysis using a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 2 kDa so that excess NaOH will be filtered out. After dialysis, syringe filtration was done in order to further purify the OGQD solution. After all the steps were executed, 2 wt% of OGQD was acquired and stored in the refrigerator until further use.

The polyamide Nylon-6,6 MF membrane was placed on a pressure-assisted filtration device followed by pouring a 20 ml of freshly sonicated GO or GO-OGQD aqueous dispersion into the column of the device. A pressure of 5 kg/cm2 was used and maintained to compress and stack the GO sheets onto the MF membrane. All membranes were oven-dried at 50 °C for at least 6 h to undergo thermal annealing of the GO or GO-OGQD. After that, all membranes were vacuum dried for 3 h to ensure complete dryness before testing in pervaporation to ensure most water or moisture are removed from the membrane.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai™ G2 F-20S-TWIN) was used in order to characterize the particles of OGQD by preparing and sonicating OGQD solution and dropped onto a copper grid with carbon mesh followed by vacuum drying overnight before the actual TEM test. FTIR (Spectrum One, Perkin Elmer) and XPS analysis (Thermo Scientific, K-Alpha) was used in determining the chemical compositions of the fabricated membranes and the elemental composition of prepared OGQD solution, respectively.

For the morphology characterizations, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Model S-4800 SEM, Hitachi) was performed in order to determine the changes in thickness of the GO layers and GO-OGQD layers on the prepared membranes. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Model D8 Advance Eco, Bruker) was performed to determine the 2θ and the d-spacing of GO laminates on the prepared membranes. The d-spacing of the GO laminates is the major characteristic that is needed to be determined in this study.

Butanol dehydration separation was conducted via pervaporation membrane separation technology. The mechanism of pervaporation is correlated with the solution-diffusion model. The pervaporation was done using a home-designed pervaporation set-up as described previously [28]. Briefly, the feed mixture and its temperature were flowed and controlled through the membrane cell using a circulating thermostatic bath at a flow rate of 7 L min−1. The effective membrane area was 4.91 cm2. The permeate was controlled under vacuum conditions using a vacuum pump at 0.3 kPa. The permeate vapor was collected in a small cold-trap condensed using liquid nitrogen. Finally, the feed and permeate composition was determined using gas chromatography (GC, China Chromatography Personal GC-1000) equipped with a TCD and Porapak Q column. In this test, total flux of permeate (J, g m-2h−1) and separation factor (αPV) were calculated based on the following equations:
(1)
J = W / A t
(2)
Î± PV
=
Y water
/ " Y " _ " a l c o h o l "
X water
/
X alcohol

where W is the weight in grams (g) of the permeate collected after the sampling time interval t (h) at the membrane area A (m2). Y and X are the mass fractions of water and alcohol in the permeate and feed side, respectively.

Pervaporation performances of different specific deposition of GO were first tested to optimize the concentration of the best pristine membrane before proceeding to the addition of OGQD. After this, different concentrations of GO-OGQD were tested for the optimization of the modified membrane which is important for the succeeding PV experiment.

Variations of feed temperatures were also tested in PV ranging from 25 °C, 40 °C, 55 °C, and 70 °C. These were done to test the stability of the membrane towards different temperatures, especially at a higher one.

The concentration ratio of n-butanol and water was also varied: 95/5 wt%, 90/10 wt%, 85/15 wt%, and 80/20 wt%. This was performed to ensure the stability of the membrane and to know the potential of the best membranes in separating n-butanol mixtures. Different butanol isomers (iso-BuOH, sec-BuOH, and tert-BuOH) were also used as feed in the pervaporation experiment of the best membranes. This was performed to know the efficiency of the best membranes in dehydrating different butanol isomers. Lastly, the stability performance of the best-modified membrane was determined by doing a long-term operation. In this, the membranes were continuously tested in pervaporation for 2 weeks straight.

Furthermore, the XPS narrow scan spectrum of OGQD is shown in Fig. 2 a to show the functional groups present in the chemical structure of OGQD which was found similar to our previous studies. It also possesses the same functional group as GO. Four kinds of carbon bonds were observed: C-C (284.0 eV), C-O (285.5 eV), C = O (287.3 eV), and O-C = O (288.4 eV). High peak values of C-C bond and C-O bond could indicate the incomplete carbonization of citric acid [23,33,34]. To further prove the interaction between GO and OGQDs, XPS wide scan analysis of both GO and GO-OGQD100 is shown in Fig. 2 b. It showed that with upon addition of OGQD, the oxygen to carbon atomic ratio of the GO-OGQD100 membrane decreased from 0.68 to 0.61 denoting the binding and interaction of carbon and oxygen-containing functional groups between the two carbon-based nanomaterials. In addition, the oxygen atomic percentage noticeably increased from 35.85% to 37.73%. This could be caused by the addition of more oxygen-containing functional groups across the regions of GO from the OGQD [23]. Taking altogether, the results gathered from FTIR and XPS analysis, the slight shift in both unoxidized regions and hydroxyl FTIR peaks and the increase in O/C ratio in the membranes loaded with OGQDs indicate the possible physical interaction between GO and OGQD through π-π interaction and H-bonding. Thus, a physical cross-linking could have occurred between GO and OGQD.

In a molecular dynamics simulation for GO-based membranes for separating ethanol/water mixtures it was found that a higher O/C ratio strengthens the affinity between water and membrane. In addition, a high oxidation degree leads to the preferential adsorption of water molecules which also takes an important role in water permeation process [35]. The increase of O/C ratio for GO-OGQD100 membranes compared to the pristine GO membrane could be beneficial for water adsorption.

The cross-sectional images of the GO and GO-OGQD membranes were observed through SEM and is shown in Fig. 3 . A noticeable change in the thickness of the membrane could be observed. Firstly, all of the OGQD loaded membranes appeared to be thinner compared with the pristine GO membrane that has a thickness of 1.28 ± 0.04 μm. Secondly, the thickness of the stacked layers for the GO-OGQD from 10 ppm to 100 ppm had a decreasing trend from 1.07 ± 0.07 μm to 0.72 ± 0.09 μm, respectively. It is proposed in Fig. 4 that the integration of OGQDs onto the nanosheets may have filled up the wrinkled gaps on the GO nanosheets leading to the well-stacked laminates. This could be the reason why the thickness declined with upon the addition of OGQDs. Lastly, the thickness increased to 1.19 ± 0.11 μm at the highest loading of OGQD at 200 ppm which could be caused by the aggregation of OGQDs. The changes in the thickness of the GO layer are an indication of the physical cross-linking that may have occurred between GO nanosheets with OGQDs particles. The water contact angle measurements of the membranes ( Figure S1 ) revealed a decreasing trend from 39.4° to 34.4° when the OGQD loading was increased from 0 to 100 ppm respectively. This proved that more hydrophilic sites were present on the surface of the membrane when OGQD was integrated in the membrane further improving affinity towards water. While the contact angle increased to 40.6° at 200 ppm OGQD loading possibly due to agglomeration of the particles. In relation to the decline in contact angle measurements, the membrane surface roughness had increased upon OGQD integration when compared to pristine GO membrane proving higher effective membrane area thereby giving the possibility to increase the permeation flux ( Figure S2 ).

As shown previously in our study, the interlayer space distance of GO-based membranes is an important criterion to explain the separation efficiency [21,36]. Thus, the d-spacing of GO and the GO-OGQD membranes were identified using XRD spectroscopy in both dry and wet state conditions shown in Fig. 5 . Under dry state condition, the pristine GO membrane has a d-spacing of 8.59 Å and it decreased as the peak shifts to a higher 2θ (Fig. 5 a) upon the addition of OGQD. The d-spacing of the GO-OGQD membranes decreased from 8.55 Å to 8.43 Å from 10 ppm to 50 ppm loading. Then it slightly increased for GO-OGQD100 and GO-OGQD200 at 8.47 Å. The resulting smaller d-spacing for the GO-OGQD membranes further proved the enhanced interactions between nanosheets brought by the physical cross-linking. Then the membranes were soaked in 90/10 wt% n-butanol/water (n-BuOH/water) mixture for 24 h followed by the measurement of interlayer space by XRD. Under wet state condition, all of the membranes showed an increase in d-spacing as the 2θ shifted to a lower value (Fig. 5 b) which is typical since the solvent can fill up the interlayer space between nanosheets. Another fact that can be related is the increase in O/C ratio after OGQD integration, it may enhance the water adsorption and strengthen the affinity between water and membranes [35]. This effect may lead to water molecules to filling-up the interlayer space thus an increase in d-spacing.

Interestingly, unlike in our previous findings where poly(vinyl alcohol) was used to intercalate the GO nanosheets [21], the results herein showed an increasing difference in the d-spacing between the dry and wet state counterparts (Fig. 5 c). This possibly was due to that only physical interaction happened between GO and OGQD and no stronger covalent bonds were formed. It was not enough to fully restrict the expansion of GO nanosheets under wet state conditions. Nevertheless, the expansion of the interlayer space may be beneficial to the enhancement of total flux or the water selectivity as long as the space is small enough to reject the larger alcohol molecules and only permit the transport of the smaller water molecules. The calculated d-spacing and differences between dry and wet state condition is shown in Table S1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pervaporation separation performance of the composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To visualize the transport process that may have occurred a schematic representation of the diffusion of water and n-butanol within the nanosheets is shown in Fig. 7 . As can be visualized, the pristine GO membrane has wrinkled gaps that may appear to increase the flux but the n-butanol molecules can block these gaps resulting to a lower flux. As for the GO-OGQD100 membrane, the elimination of the wrinkled gaps by OGQD inhibited the alcohol molecules entering and allowed more water molecules to permeate and through the sheets that was further oxidized. Hence, the water selectivity is enhanced due to the presence of OGQD.

The variation of pervaporation operating conditions to evaluate the efficiency of the membrane was done with GO-OGQD100 membrane. The effect of feed operating temperature in the separation of 90/10 wt% n-BuOH/water mixture using GO-OGQD100 membranes is shown in Fig. 8 . The total flux increased from 945 g m-2h−1 at 25 °C to 1563 g m-2h−1 at 70 °C (Fig. 8 a). The total flux rapidly increased after 40 °C due to the enhanced driving force caused by the increase in partial pressure of permeating molecules in the feed side while the permeate side was maintained at vacuum condition. Also, the change in temperature directly affected the mobility of the permeating molecules and increasing their permeation across the membrane. This effect caused the water concentration to drop from 99.99 wt% at 25 °C to 99.31 wt% at 70 °C. The slight drop in the water concentration in permeate could be seen by the increase in the n-BuOH flux when the temperature was increased (Fig. 8 b). However, the water flux is still way higher compared to n-BuOH flux showing that the membrane still preferentially allows most water molecules to permeate and creating enough barrier to block the n-BuOH molecules. Moreover, the efficient separation at 25 °C is an indication that the process could be operated even without applying excess heat in the feed.

The effect of feed composition on the performance of the homostructured nanocomposite GO-OGQD100 membrane was done by varying the water content in the feed from 5 wt% to 20 wt% at 25 °C as shown in Fig. 9 . The total flux increased from 443 g m-2h−1 to 1905 g m-2h−1 when the water concentration in feed was raised from 5 wt% to 20 wt%. While the water concentration in permeate decreased from 99.94 wt% to 99.56 wt%. This was caused by the expansion of free volume spaces due to swelling. The selective layer swells at high water content thus allowing the permeation of both water and butanol molecules through the membrane. Nevertheless, the membrane still showed good stability even at 20 wt% water in feed since a high-water concentration in permeate is achieved. This is an indication that water molecules transport faster than the butanol molecules. Simply, the membrane creates a good barrier for the organic solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pervaporation separation performance with butanol isomer/water mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The homostrucutred nanocomposite GO-OGQD100 membrane was also used to separate all of the four-butanol isomer/water mixtures (i.e. n-butanol, iso-butanol, sec-butanol, and tert-butanol) as shown in Fig. 10 . The total flux was found to decrease with the more sterically hindered the structure is. Meaning, the flux decreased from linear to branched structures. The trend for the total flux is as follows: n-butanol > iso-butanol > sec-butanol > tert-butanol. While the water concentration in permeate did not greatly dropped ranging from 99.95 to 99.99 wt%. Showing the membrane provided a good butanol molecule barrier and only allows water to permeate. Thus, focusing on the total flux, the trend could be related with the molar volume and average dynamic cross-section listed in Table S2 . That is, a faster permeation will be expected for a permeating molecule with a smaller molar volume and average dynamic cross-section. Therefore, for example, the tert-butanol has the largest average dynamic cross-section compared to the other isomers thus having the lowest total flux. The sterically hindered branched structure of tert-butanol has more resistance during permeation across the membrane. The trend of the flux can also be related and supported by the differences in solubility parameter values of the permeating molecules listed in Table S3 . The solubility parameter values were found to decrease with the branching of the butanol isomer. The tert-butanol appeared to have the largest difference with water molecules and this means it has lower affinity with water molecules. The weak affinity between the permeating molecules led to low solvation during the separation process. A similar trend was previously observed with polymeric thin-film membranes integrated with nitrogen-doped quantum dots [37] and commercial PV membrane from Sulzer Pervap® 2510 [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term operating performance of the composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the homostructured nanocomposite GO-OGQD100 membrane has shown exceptional stability under long-term operation for 336 h at 25 °C in separating 90/10 wt% n-BuOH/water mixture (Fig. 11 ). The total flux can be maintained at around 950 g m-2h−1 with a water concentration in permeate ranging from 98 to 99 wt%. The long-term test shows that the membrane can withstand extended operation use of the membrane and that GO-based membranes have the potential use for an efficient and stable selective layer in membrane separation technologies. Additionally, the OGQD can be an alternative material to stabilize carbon-based membranes. Altogether, the homostructured nanocomposite membrane herein could provide insights and understanding in designing membranes combining 2-dimensional and zero-dimensional nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of pervaporation performances with other membranes reported
ce:section.ce:para: The n-butanol dehydration performance of GO-OGQD100 membrane was compared to other membranes used in separating high n-butanol feed content through pervaporation (Table 1 ). The GO-OGQD100 membrane offered an improved concentration of water in the permeate while having a considerable flux, taking note that the selective layer is mostly composed of 2D lamellae and the separation was done at unheated feed environment (25 °C). The other membranes reported in literature are mostly polymeric membranes or hybrid membranes work better at higher feed temperature resulting to higher permeation flux. However, the GO-OGQD100 membrane can also be operated at higher temperatures and minimally sacrifices the water concentration in permeate. Also, the incorporation of OGQD enhanced both permeation flux and selectivity when compared to the pristine GO membrane.

Taking advantage of the structure and chemical functional groups similarity between GO and OGQDs a homostructured nanocomposite membrane was developed for the dehydration of butanol/water mixture. The interactions between GO and OGQD were mostly by π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding brought by the physical cross-linking. The addition of OGQD into the GO lamellae resulted to a thinner separation layer that was the result of the OGQD eliminating the wrinkled gaps between GO nanosheets. At 100 ppm of OGQD, the GO lamellae thickness dropped to 0.72 ± 0.09 µm from the pristine GO with 1.28 ± 0.04 µm. The changes in the thickness was also associated to the trend in flux where the GO-OGQD100 has thinnest layer with the highest total flux in separating 90/10 wt% n-BuOH/water mixture at 25 °C. The total flux was 945 g m-2h−1 with a water concentration in permeate of 99.99 wt%. The high-water selectivity of the membrane can be directly associated to the “empty space” within the nanosheets as it appeared to be small enough to block the n-butanol molecules and allow the transport of the smaller water molecules. The GO-OGQD100 membrane also showed good stability at a wide range of feed operating condition. At increasing feed temperature and water content in feed, the homostructured nanocomposite membrane provided high water selectivity > 99 wt% owing to the improved water molecule attraction by OGQD nanoparticles. Furthermore, the membrane was also stable at long-term operation where it maintained the flux and selectivity for 336 h. The results in this study can provide insights in designing carbon-based membranes by combining both 2-dimensional and zero-dimensional nanomaterials having similar structure and chemical functional groups.

Rumwald Leo G. Lecaros: Data curation, Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Andrea R. Matira: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization. Lemmuel L. Tayo: Supervision. Wei-Song Hung: Supervision. Chien-Chieh Hu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Hui-An Tsai: Supervision. Kueir-Rarn Lee: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Juin-Yih Lai: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to sincerely thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108-2218-E-033-007-MY3 and MOST 109-2811-E-011-513) for financially supporting this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.120166.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
